# Inference Efficiency {.section-divider}

## Why Speed Matters in Robot Control

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Control loops have strict timing budgets.
- Slow inference increases tracking error and reaction delay.
- Contact-rich tasks may require fast corrections.
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Control-loop latency budget</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/control_loop_latency.png</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: latency is a control variable, not only a software performance metric.

Intuition: if the robot reacts late to contact, even a good planned action may arrive after the physical situation has changed.

Warning: high average frame rate is not enough; worst-case timing and jitter matter in control loops.
:::

---

## Denoising Steps vs Latency

Let $K$ be the number of denoising steps. A rough latency model is:

$$
T_{infer} \approx K \cdot T_{net}
$$

where $T_{net}$ is the time for one denoising network evaluation.

::: {.notes}
Main point: the number of denoising steps multiplies the cost of one network pass.

Intuition: if one denoising step takes 5 ms, 20 steps already cost about 100 ms before accounting for sensing and control overhead.

Warning: this equation is a simplification; batching, GPU synchronization, encoders, and communication can dominate in practice.
:::

---

## DDIM and Fewer Sampling Steps

- DDIM-style sampling can reduce the number of denoising steps.
- Fewer steps reduce latency.
- The risk is lower action quality or less stable behavior.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Quality-latency curve</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/sampling_steps_latency.png</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: fewer sampling steps are attractive, but they change the learned policy's behavior.

Intuition: taking fewer denoising steps is like making larger jumps during refinement; it may be faster but less precise.

Warning: do not evaluate samplers only by visual smoothness or offline loss; test closed-loop behavior on the task.
:::

---

## Action Chunking and Replanning Frequency

- Larger chunks amortize inference cost over multiple control steps.
- Smaller execution horizons react faster to new observations.
- The design choice depends on task dynamics and contact uncertainty.

::: {.notes}
Main point: chunk size and replanning frequency are a latency-reactivity tradeoff.

Intuition: slow, predictable tasks can tolerate longer chunks; fast contact changes require more frequent replanning.

Warning: executing too much of an old chunk can turn a closed-loop policy into an effectively open-loop controller.
:::

---

## Pruning and Distillation

Placeholder topics:

- smaller denoising networks
- policy distillation
- consistency-style sampling
- hardware-aware deployment

---

## Slow-Fast Policy Placeholder

Potential architecture:

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- a slow diffusion policy proposes temporally coherent plans
- a fast local controller tracks, filters, or corrects actions
- compliance parameters adapt at a higher control rate
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Slow-fast control architecture</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/slow_fast_policy.png</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: separate high-level trajectory generation from faster local correction.

Intuition: the diffusion model can plan at a slower rate while an admittance or impedance layer reacts quickly to forces.

Warning: the interface between slow and fast layers must be designed carefully; otherwise the layers can fight each other.
:::
